audioscript.text: Part 1, you will hear two friends talking about a guitar group.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 6.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 1 to 6.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hi, Coleman.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: How are you?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Good, thanks.
audioscript.speaker: COLEMAN

audioscript.text: I wanted to have a chat with you, because our friend Josh told me that you've joined a guitar group.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And it sounds interesting.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I'd really like to learn myself.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Why don't you come along?
audioscript.speaker: COLEMAN

audioscript.text: I'm sure there's room for another person.
audioscript.speaker: COLEMAN

audioscript.text: Really?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: So, who runs the classes?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: He's called a coordinator.
audioscript.speaker: COLEMAN

audioscript.text: His name's Gary Mathieson.
audioscript.speaker: COLEMAN

audioscript.text: Let me note that down.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Gary, how do you spell his surname?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: It's MATHIESON.
audioscript.speaker: COLEMAN

audioscript.text: Right, thanks.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: He's retired actually, but he's a really nice guy.
audioscript.speaker: COLEMAN

audioscript.text: And he used to play in a lot of bands.
audioscript.speaker: COLEMAN

audioscript.text: Thanks.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: So, how long have you been going?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: About a month now.
audioscript.speaker: COLEMAN

audioscript.text: And could you play anything before you started?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I knew a few chords, but that's all.
audioscript.speaker: COLEMAN

audioscript.text: I'm sure everyone will be better than me.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That's what I thought too.
audioscript.speaker: COLEMAN

audioscript.text: When I first spoke to Gary on the phone, he said it was a class for beginners, but I was still worried that everyone would be better than me.
audioscript.speaker: COLEMAN

audioscript.text: But we were all equally hopeless.
audioscript.speaker: COLEMAN

audioscript.text: Oh, that's reassuring.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: So, where do you meet?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Well, when I joined the group, they were meeting in Gary's home.
audioscript.speaker: COLEMAN

audioscript.text: But as the group got bigger, he decided to book a room at the college in town.
audioscript.speaker: COLEMAN

audioscript.text: I prefer going there.
audioscript.speaker: COLEMAN

audioscript.text: I know that place.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I used to go to tap dancing classes there when I was at secondary school.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I haven't been since though, and I can't remember what road it's in.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Is it Lock Street?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: It's just beyond there, at the bottom of New Street, near the city roundabout.
audioscript.speaker: COLEMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, of course.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: The guitar club is on the first floor, in room T347.
audioscript.speaker: COLEMAN

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And when do you meet?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Is it at the weekend?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: We meet on Thursdays.
audioscript.speaker: COLEMAN

audioscript.text: It used to be 10:30 and that suited me well, but now we meet at 11.
audioscript.speaker: COLEMAN

audioscript.text: The class that's in there before us asked if they could have the room for another 30 minutes.
audioscript.speaker: COLEMAN

audioscript.text: Oh, I see.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Well, I'd love to come.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: But I don't have a guitar.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Well, you can always buy a second-hand one.
audioscript.speaker: COLEMAN

audioscript.text: There's a website called The Perfect Instrument.
audioscript.speaker: COLEMAN

audioscript.text: That sells all kinds of guitars, violins, and so on.
audioscript.speaker: COLEMAN

audioscript.text: I'm sure you'll find something there.
audioscript.speaker: COLEMAN

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 7 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 7 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So, what's a typical lesson like with Gary?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Well, he always starts by getting us to tune our guitars.
audioscript.speaker: COLEMAN

audioscript.text: That takes about 5 minutes.
audioscript.speaker: COLEMAN

audioscript.text: Uh huh.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Some people have an app they use, but others do it by ear.
audioscript.speaker: COLEMAN

audioscript.text: Gary goes round and helps them.
audioscript.speaker: COLEMAN

audioscript.text: And while he's doing that, he tells us what he's going to do during the lesson.
audioscript.speaker: COLEMAN

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: First, we usually spend about 10 minutes doing some strumming.
audioscript.speaker: COLEMAN

audioscript.text: So, is that using oh, what are they called plectrums?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Ha, no, we just use our thumbs.
audioscript.speaker: COLEMAN

audioscript.text: Ha, much easier.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Gary reminds us where to put our fingers for each chord, and then we play them together.
audioscript.speaker: COLEMAN

audioscript.text: Sometimes we all just start laughing.
audioscript.speaker: COLEMAN

audioscript.text: Because we're so bad at keeping time.
audioscript.speaker: COLEMAN

audioscript.text: So Gary starts clapping to help us.
audioscript.speaker: COLEMAN

audioscript.text: Do you learn to play any songs?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: COLEMAN

audioscript.text: We do at least one song with words and chords.
audioscript.speaker: COLEMAN

audioscript.text: I mean, that's harder than you think.
audioscript.speaker: COLEMAN

audioscript.text: Ha, oh, I'm sure it is.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That part of the lesson takes about 15 minutes.
audioscript.speaker: COLEMAN

audioscript.text: He often brings a recording of the song, and plays it to us first.
audioscript.speaker: COLEMAN

audioscript.text: Then he hands out the song, and if there's a new chord in it, we practice that before we play it together, but really slowly.
audioscript.speaker: COLEMAN

audioscript.text: Do you do any finger picking?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That's the last 10 minutes of the lesson, when we pick out the individual notes from a tune he's made up.
audioscript.speaker: COLEMAN

audioscript.text: It's always quite simple.
audioscript.speaker: COLEMAN

audioscript.text: That must be hard though.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: It is.
audioscript.speaker: COLEMAN

audioscript.text: But people like it because they can really concentrate.
audioscript.speaker: COLEMAN

audioscript.text: And if we're all playing well, it sounds quite impressive.
audioscript.speaker: COLEMAN

audioscript.text: The only trouble is that he sometimes gets us to play one at a time.
audioscript.speaker: COLEMAN

audioscript.text: You know, alone.
audioscript.speaker: COLEMAN

audioscript.text: Oh, that's scary.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Hmm, it is, but I've got used to it now.
audioscript.speaker: COLEMAN

audioscript.text: At the end he spends about five minutes telling us what to practice for the following week.
audioscript.speaker: COLEMAN

audioscript.text: Well, thanks Coleman.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I'll go and have a look at that website I think.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: A typical 45-minute guitar lesson

audioscript.text: Part 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a man called David talking on the radio about his work as a lifeboat volunteer.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 16.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 16.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: I never really planned to be a lifeboat volunteer when I came to live in Northsea.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I'd been working in London as a website designer, but although that was interesting,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I didn't like city life.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I'd been really keen on boats as a teenager, and I thought if I went to live by the sea, I might be able to pursue that interest a bit more in my free time.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Then, I found that the Lifeboat Institution was looking for volunteers, so I decided to apply.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The Lifeboat Institution building here in Northsea's hard to miss.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's one of the largest in the country.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It was built 15 years ago, with funds provided by a generous member of the public who'd lived here all her life.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As the Lifeboat Institution is a charity that relies on that kind of donation, rather than funding provided by the government, that was a huge help to us.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: When I applied, I had to have a health assessment.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The doctors were particularly interested in my vision.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I used to be short sighted, so I'd had to wear glasses, but I'd had laser eye surgery two years earlier, so that was OK.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They gave me tests for colour blindness, and they thought I might have a problem there, but it turned out I was OK.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: When the coastguard gets an alert, all the volunteers are contacted and rush to the lifeboat station.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Our target's to get there in five minutes.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Then we try to get the boat off the dock and out to sea in another six to eight minutes.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Our team's proud that we usually achieve that.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The average time across the country's eight and a half minutes.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I've recently qualified as what's called a helmsman, which means I have the ultimate responsibility for the lifeboat.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I have to check that the equipment we use is in working order.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The crew have special life jackets that can support up to four people in the water, and it's ultimately my decision whether it's safe to launch the boat.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But it's very rare not to launch it, even in the worst weather.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As well as going out on the lifeboat, my work involves other things too.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: A lot of people underestimate how quickly conditions can change at sea, so I speak to youth groups and sailing clubs in the area.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: About the sorts of problems that sailors and swimmers can have, if the weather suddenly gets bad.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We also have a lot of volunteers who organize activities to raise money for us.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And we couldn't manage without them.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 17 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 17 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: The training we get is a continuous process, focusing on technical competence and safe handling techniques, and it's given me the confidence to deal with extreme situations without panicking.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I was glad I'd done a first aid course before I started, as that's a big help with the casualty care activities we do.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We've done a lot on how to deal with ropes and tie knots - that's an essential skill.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: After a year, I did a one-week residential course.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Led by specialists, they had a wave-tank where they could create extreme weather conditions - so we could get experience at what to do if the boat turned over in a storm at night, for example.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Since I started, I've had to deal with a range of emergency situations, but the work's hugely motivating.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's not just about saving lives.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I've learned a lot about the technology involved.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: My background in IT's been useful here, and I can use my expertise to help other volunteers.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They're a great group.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We're like a family really, which helps when you're dragging yourself out of bed on a cold stormy night.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But actually, it's the colder months that can be the most rewarding time.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That's when the incidents tend to be more serious, and you realize that you can make a huge difference to the outcome.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So, if any of you listeners are interested.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Why don't you give us a call...
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have 30 seconds to check your answers to part 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: The doctors were particularly interested in my vision.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: I used to be short sighted, so I'd had to wear glasses, but I'd had laser eye surgery two years earlier, so that was OK.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They gave me tests for colour blindness, and they thought I might have a problem there, but it turned out I was OK.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: When the coastguard gets an alert, all the volunteers are contacted and rush to the lifeboat station.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Our target's to get there in five minutes.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Then we try to get the boat off the dock and out to sea in another six to eight minutes.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Our team's proud that we usually achieve that.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The average time across the country's eight and a half minutes.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: I've recently qualified as what's called a helmsman, which means I have the ultimate responsibility for the lifeboat.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: I have to check that the equipment we use is in working order.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The crew have special life jackets that can support up to four people in the water, and it's ultimately my decision whether it's safe to launch the boat.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But it's very rare not to launch it, even in the worst weather.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: As well as going out on the lifeboat, my work involves other things too.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: A lot of people underestimate how quickly conditions can change at sea, so I speak to youth groups and sailing clubs in the area.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: About the sorts of problems that sailors and swimmers can have, if the weather suddenly gets bad.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We also have a lot of volunteers who organize activities to raise money for us.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And we couldn't manage without them.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 17 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen and answer questions 17 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: The training we get is a continuous process, focusing on technical competence and safe handling techniques, and it's given me the confidence to deal with extreme situations without panicking.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: I was glad I'd done a first aid course before I started, as that's a big help with the casualty care activities we do.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We've done a lot on how to deal with ropes and tie knots - that's an essential skill.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: After a year, I did a one-week residential course.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Led by specialists, they had a wave-tank where they could create extreme weather conditions - so we could get experience at what to do if the boat turned over in a storm at night, for example.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Since I started, I've had to deal with a range of emergency situations, but the work's hugely motivating.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's not just about saving lives.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: I've learned a lot about the technology involved.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: My background in IT's been useful here, and I can use my expertise to help other volunteers.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They're a great group.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We're like a family really, which helps when you're dragging yourself out of bed on a cold stormy night.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But actually, it's the colder months that can be the most rewarding time.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That's when the incidents tend to be more serious, and you realize that you can make a huge difference to the outcome.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So, if any of you listeners are interested.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Why don't you give us a call...
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of part 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have 30 seconds to check your answers to part 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: What made David leave London and move to Northsea?
options: He was eager to develop a hobby.
He wanted to work shorter hours.
He found his job in website design unsatisfying.

items.title: The Lifeboat Institution in Northsea was built with money provided by
options: a local organisation.
a local resident.
the local council.

items.title: In his health assessment, the doctor was concerned about the fact that David
options: might be colour blind.
was rather short-sighted.
had undergone eye surgery.

items.title: After arriving at the lifeboat station, they aim to launch the boat within
options: five minutes.
six to eight minutes.
eight and a half minutes.

items.title: As a 'helmsman', David has the responsibility of deciding
options: who will be the members of his crew.
what equipment it will be necessary to take.
if the lifeboat should be launched.

items.title: As well as going out on the lifeboat, David
options: gives talks on safety at sea.
helps with fundraising.
recruits new volunteers.

body.title: Which TWO things does David say about the lifeboat volunteer training?
options: The residential course developed his leadership skills.
The training in use of ropes and knots was quite brief.
The training exercises have built up his mental strength.
The casualty care activities were particularly challenging for him.
The wave tank activities provided practice in survival techniques.

body.title: Which TWO things does David find most motivating about the work he does?
options: working as part of a team
experiences when working in winter
being thanked by those he has helped
the fact that it keeps him fit
the chance to develop new equipment

audioscript.text: Part 3, you will hear two students called Bella and Don, discussing a presentation they plan to do on recycling footwear.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First you have some time to look at questions 21 to 24.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 24.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hi, Don.
audioscript.speaker: BELLA

audioscript.text: Did you get the copy of the article on recycling footwear that I emailed you?
audioscript.speaker: BELLA

audioscript.text: Yeah, it's here.
audioscript.speaker: DON

audioscript.text: I've had a look at it.
audioscript.speaker: DON

audioscript.text: So, do you think it's a good topic for our presentation?
audioscript.speaker: BELLA

audioscript.text: Well, before I started reading it, I thought recycling footwear.
audioscript.speaker: DON

audioscript.text: Well, although it's quite interesting, perhaps there isn't enough to say about it.
audioscript.speaker: DON

audioscript.text: 'Cause we put shoes in recycling bins, they go to charity shops, and that's about it.
audioscript.speaker: DON

audioscript.text: But there's much more to it than that.
audioscript.speaker: BELLA

audioscript.text: I realize that now.
audioscript.speaker: DON

audioscript.text: And I'm keen to research the topic more.
audioscript.speaker: DON

audioscript.text: That's great.
audioscript.speaker: BELLA

audioscript.text: One of the things I didn't realize until I read the article was just how many pairs of trainers get recycled.
audioscript.speaker: DON

audioscript.text: Well, a lot of young people wear them all the time now.
audioscript.speaker: BELLA

audioscript.text: They've become more popular than ordinary shoes.
audioscript.speaker: BELLA

audioscript.text: I know, I guess they are very hard wearing.
audioscript.speaker: DON

audioscript.text: But don't they look a bit casual for school uniform?
audioscript.speaker: DON

audioscript.text: I don't think they're right for that.
audioscript.speaker: DON

audioscript.text: Actually, I think some of them look quite smart on pupils, better than a scruffy old pair of shoes.
audioscript.speaker: BELLA

audioscript.text: So, do you keep shoes a long time?
audioscript.speaker: BELLA

audioscript.text: Yes, though I do tend to wear my old pairs for doing dirty jobs like cleaning my bike.
audioscript.speaker: DON

audioscript.text: I must admit, I've recycled some perfectly good shoes that haven't gone out of fashion and still fit, just because they don't look great on me anymore.
audioscript.speaker: BELLA

audioscript.text: That's awful, isn't it?
audioscript.speaker: BELLA

audioscript.text: I think it's common because there's so much choice.
audioscript.speaker: DON

audioscript.text: The article did say that recent sales of footwear have increased enormously.
audioscript.speaker: DON

audioscript.text: That didn't surprise me.
audioscript.speaker: BELLA

audioscript.text: No.
audioscript.speaker: DON

audioscript.text: But then it said that the amount of recycled footwear has fallen, it's 6% now, compared to a previous level of 11% that doesn't seem to make sense.
audioscript.speaker: DON

audioscript.text: That's because not everything goes through the recycling process.
audioscript.speaker: BELLA

audioscript.text: Some footwear just isn't good enough to resell for one reason or another, and gets rejected.
audioscript.speaker: BELLA

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the discussion, you have some time to look at questions 25 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 25 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So, let's find some examples in the article of footwear that was rejected for recycling.
audioscript.speaker: BELLA

audioscript.text: OK, I think there are some in the interview with the recycling manager.
audioscript.speaker: DON

audioscript.text: Yeah, here it is.
audioscript.speaker: DON

audioscript.text: Hmm.
audioscript.speaker: BELLA

audioscript.text: Let's start with the lady's high heeled shoes.
audioscript.speaker: BELLA

audioscript.text: What did he say about those?
audioscript.speaker: BELLA

audioscript.text: He said they were probably expensive.
audioscript.speaker: DON

audioscript.text: The material was suede, and they were beige in colour.
audioscript.speaker: DON

audioscript.text: It looked like someone had only worn them once, but in a very wet field.
audioscript.speaker: DON

audioscript.text: So the heels were too stained with MUD and grass to resell them.
audioscript.speaker: DON

audioscript.text: OK, and the leather ankle boots, what was wrong with them?
audioscript.speaker: BELLA

audioscript.text: Apparently, the heels were worn, but that wasn't the problem.
audioscript.speaker: DON

audioscript.text: One of the shoes was a much lighter shade than the other one.
audioscript.speaker: DON

audioscript.text: It had obviously been left in the sun.
audioscript.speaker: DON

audioscript.text: I suppose even second-hand shoes should look the same.
audioscript.speaker: DON

audioscript.text: Sure.
audioscript.speaker: BELLA

audioscript.text: Then there were the red baby shoes.
audioscript.speaker: BELLA

audioscript.text: Oh yes.
audioscript.speaker: DON

audioscript.text: We're told to tie shoes together when we put them in a recycling bin.
audioscript.speaker: DON

audioscript.text: People often don't bother, you'd think it would have been easy to find the other, but it wasn't.
audioscript.speaker: BELLA

audioscript.text: That was a shame because they were obviously new.
audioscript.speaker: BELLA

audioscript.text: The trainers were interesting.
audioscript.speaker: DON

audioscript.text: He said they looked like they'd been worn by a marathon runner.
audioscript.speaker: DON

audioscript.text: Yeah, weren't they split?
audioscript.speaker: BELLA

audioscript.text: Not exactly.
audioscript.speaker: DON

audioscript.text: One of the soles was so worn under the foot that you could put your finger through it.
audioscript.speaker: DON

audioscript.text: Well, we could certainly use some of those examples in our presentation to explain why 90% of shoes that people take to recycling centers or bins,
audioscript.speaker: DON

audioscript.text: get thrown into landfill.
audioscript.speaker: DON

audioscript.text: Hmm.
audioscript.speaker: BELLA

audioscript.text: What did you think about the project his team set up to avoid this by making new shoes out of the good parts of old shoes?
audioscript.speaker: BELLA

audioscript.text: It sounded like a good idea.
audioscript.speaker: DON

audioscript.text: They get so many shoes, they should be able to match parts.
audioscript.speaker: DON

audioscript.text: I wasn't surprised that it failed though.
audioscript.speaker: DON

audioscript.text: I mean, who wants to buy second-hand shoes really?
audioscript.speaker: DON

audioscript.text: Think of all the germs you could catch.
audioscript.speaker: DON

audioscript.text: Well, people didn't refuse them for that reason, did they?
audioscript.speaker: BELLA

audioscript.text: It was because the pairs of shoes weren't identical.
audioscript.speaker: BELLA

audioscript.text: They still managed to ship them overseas though.
audioscript.speaker: DON

audioscript.text: That's another area we need to discuss.
audioscript.speaker: BELLA

audioscript.text: You know, I used to consider this topic just from my own perspective.
audioscript.speaker: DON

audioscript.text: By thinking about my own recycling behavior, without looking at the bigger picture.
audioscript.speaker: DON

audioscript.text: So much happens once shoes leave the recycling area.
audioscript.speaker: DON

audioscript.text: It's not as simple as you first think, and we can show that by taking a very different approach to it.
audioscript.speaker: BELLA

audioscript.text: Absolutely.
audioscript.speaker: DON

audioscript.text: So let's discuss...
audioscript.speaker: DON

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have 30 seconds to check your answers to part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: At first, Don thought the topic of recycling footwear might be too
options: limited in scope.
hard to research.
boring for listeners.

items.title: When discussing trainers, Bella and Don disagree about
options: how popular they are among young people.
how suitable they are for school.
how quickly they wear out.

items.title: Bella says that she sometimes recycles shoes because
options: they no longer fit.
she no longer likes them.
they are no longer in fashion.

items.title: What did the article say that confused Don?
options: Public consumption of footwear has risen.
Less footwear is recycled now than in the past.
People dispose of more footwear than they used to.

text: What reasons did the recycling manager give for rejecting footwear, according to the students?
textReadable: What reasons did the recycling manager give for rejecting footwear, according to the students?

list: the colour of one shoe had faded
one shoe had a hole in it
the stitching on the shoes was broken
body.title: Footwear

items.title: Why did the project to make 'new' shoes out of old shoes fail?
options: People believed the 'new' pairs of shoes were unhygienic.
There were not enough good parts to use in the old shoes.
The shoes in the 'new' pairs were not completely alike.

items.title: Bella and Don agree that they can present their topic
options: from a new angle.
with relevant images.
in a straightforward way.

audioscript.text: Part 4, you will hear a zoology student giving a presentation about an animal called tardigrade.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: For my project on invertebrates, I chose to study tardigrades.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: These are microscopic, or to be more precise, near microscopic animals.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There are well over 1,000 known species of these tiny animals, which belong to the phylum tardigrada.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Most tardigrades range in length from naught point naught five (0.05) to 1 millimetre.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Though the largest species can grow to be 1.2 millimeters in length, they are also sometimes called water bears, water because that's where they thrive best.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And bear because of the way they move.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Moss piglet is another name for tardigrades, because of the way they look when viewed from the front.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They were first discovered in Germany in 1773 by Johann Goeze, who coined the name "Tardigrada".
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As I say, there are many different species of tardigrade, too many to describe here, but generally speaking, the different species share similar physical traits.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They have a body which is short and also rounded, a bit like a barrel.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And the body comprises four segments, each segment has a pair of legs.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: At the end of which are between four and 8 sharp claws.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I should also say that some species don't have any claws.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: What they have are discs.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And these work by means of suction.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They enable the tardigrade to cling on to surfaces or to grip its prey.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Within the body,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: there are no lungs or any organs for breathing at all.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Instead, oxygen and also blood are transported in a fluid that fills the cavity of the body.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As far as the tardigrade's head is concerned, the best way I can describe this is that it looks rather strange - a bit squashed even - though many of the websites I looked at described its appearance as cute.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Which isn't exactly very scientific.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The tardigrade's mouth is a kind of tube that can open outwards to reveal teeth-like structures known as "stylets".
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: These are sharp enough to pierce plant or animal cells.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So where are tardigrades found?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Well, they live in every part of the world.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In a variety of habitats, most commonly on the bed of a lake, or on many kinds of plants, or in very wet environments.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There's been some interesting research which has found that tardigrades are capable of surviving radiation and very high pressure, and they're also able to withstand temperatures as cold as minus 200 degrees centigrade.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Or highs of more than 148 degrees centigrade, which is incredibly hot.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It has been said that tardigrades could survive long after human beings have been wiped out, even in the event of an asteroid hitting the earth.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: If conditions become too extreme and tardigrades are at risk of drying out, they enter a state called cryptobiosis.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They curl into a ball called a tun.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That's t u n, by retracting their head and legs, and their metabolism drops to less than 1% of normal levels.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They can remain like this until they're reintroduced to water, when they will come back to life in a matter of a few hours, while in a state of cryptobiosis.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Tardigrades produce a protein that protects their DNA.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In 2016, scientists revived two tardigrades that had been tuns for more than 30 years.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There was a report that, in 1948 a 120-year-old tun was revived, but this experiment has never been repeated.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There are currently several tests taking place in space to determine how long tardigrades might be able to survive there.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I believe the record so far is 10 days.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So moving on, in terms of their diet, tardigrades consume liquids in order to survive.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Although they have teeth, they don't use these for chewing.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They suck the juices from moss or extract fluid from seaweed, but some species prey on other tardigrades, from other species or within their own.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I suppose this isn't surprising given that tardigrades are mainly comprised of liquid and are coated with a type of gel.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Finally, I'd like to mention the conservation status of tardigrades.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It is estimated that they have been in existence for approximately half a billion years, and in that time, they have survived five mass extinctions.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So it will probably come as no surprise to you that tardigrades have not been evaluated by the International Union for conservation of Nature, and are not on any endangered list.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Some researchers have described them as thriving.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Does anyone have any questions they'd like to ask?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: That is the end of the listening test.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: In the IELTS test, you would now have 10 minutes to transfer your answers to the listening answer sheet.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Part 4, you will hear a zoology student giving a presentation about an animal called tardigrade.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: For my project on invertebrates, I chose to study tardigrades.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: These are microscopic, or to be more precise, near microscopic animals.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: There are well over 1,000 known species of these tiny animals, which belong to the phylum tardigrada.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Most tardigrades range in length from naught point naught five (0.05) to 1 millimetre.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Though the largest species can grow to be 1.2 millimeters in length, they are also sometimes called water bears, water because that's where they thrive best.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And bear because of the way they move.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Moss piglet is another name for tardigrades, because of the way they look when viewed from the front.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They were first discovered in Germany in 1773 by Johann Goeze, who coined the name "Tardigrada".
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: As I say, there are many different species of tardigrade, too many to describe here, but generally speaking, the different species share similar physical traits.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They have a body which is short and also rounded, a bit like a barrel.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And the body comprises four segments, each segment has a pair of legs.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: At the end of which are between four and 8 sharp claws.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: I should also say that some species don't have any claws.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: What they have are discs.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And these work by means of suction.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They enable the tardigrade to cling on to surfaces or to grip its prey.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Within the body,
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: there are no lungs or any organs for breathing at all.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Instead, oxygen and also blood are transported in a fluid that fills the cavity of the body.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: As far as the tardigrade's head is concerned, the best way I can describe this is that it looks rather strange - a bit squashed even - though many of the websites I looked at described its appearance as cute.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Which isn't exactly very scientific.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The tardigrade's mouth is a kind of tube that can open outwards to reveal teeth-like structures known as "stylets".
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: These are sharp enough to pierce plant or animal cells.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So where are tardigrades found?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Well, they live in every part of the world.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In a variety of habitats, most commonly on the bed of a lake, or on many kinds of plants, or in very wet environments.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: There's been some interesting research which has found that tardigrades are capable of surviving radiation and very high pressure, and they're also able to withstand temperatures as cold as minus 200 degrees centigrade.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Or highs of more than 148 degrees centigrade, which is incredibly hot.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.speaker: SPEAKER
